extra.reasoning: The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds on all reachable executions. sent is set true only by t2, whose guard is `not opted_out`, so sent becomes true only while opted_out is false. The sole writer of opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which atomically also sets sent:=false. Once opted_out is true, nothing ever resets it to false, and t2 (the only sent:=true transition) is disabled because its guard `not opted_out` fails. The other events available while opted_out is true—optout (t1), send_attempt under opted_out (t3), and tick (t4)—all set sent:=false. Therefore every reachable state with opted_out=true necessarily has sent=false. Exhaustively: the only candidate violating state pattern is (opted_out=T, sent=T), and no transition can produce it (t1/t3/t4 force sent=F; t2 forces opted_out=F). It is unreachable within any number of events, including the ≤5 bound. Property holds; no counterexample exists.
